people.bio: Marriott Ogle Tarbotton (b. 1835; d. 1887) was one of the most distinguished municipal engineers of Victorian England. He started his career in Wakefield. In 1859 he was appointed Nottingham's Borough Surveyor, and Engineer to the Waterworks Committee in 1880. He carried out many important projects but is especially remembered for replacing the old bridge over the River Trent with the structure that stands today (widened in 1926, but the original portion can still be identified from the water). He also started Nottingham's modern sewerage system, establishing Stoke Bardolph Sewerage Disposal Works. He was the first municipal engineer in the world to employ subways under streets to carry public services (gas, sewers, etc.), a system incorporated when Victoria Street was created. He built the University College in Shakespeare Street, Nottingham (now part of the Trent Polytechnic) and laid the foundation for the present inner city boulevards. He designed Papplewick Pumping Station, one of the finest examples of Victorian engineering. His initials MOT and his wife's initials EOT appear in the stained glass windows of 6 South Road, which was built for him.
Emily Tarbotton was the wife of Marriott Ogle Tarbotton. Her initials EOT appear in the stained glass windows of 6 South Road alongside her husband's MOT.

significant_places.name: Papplewick Pumping Station
significant_places.description: Papplewick Pumping Station was designed by Marriott Ogle Tarbotton, Borough Surveyor of Nottingham, and opened in 1884. It is one of the finest examples of Victorian engineering in England, built to supply clean water to Nottingham. The initials MOT (Marriott Ogle Tarbotton) appear in the stained glass windows of the pumping station, matching those found in the windows of his home at 6 South Road.

person_connections.connection: Designer of Papplewick Pumping Station; his initials MOT appear in its stained glass windows

significant_places.name: Trent Bridge, Nottingham
significant_places.description: The current Trent Bridge was designed by Marriott Ogle Tarbotton, Borough Surveyor of Nottingham, replacing the old bridge over the River Trent. The original structure, widened in 1926, can still be identified from beneath.

significant_places.name: Stoke Bardolph Sewage Works
significant_places.description: Stoke Bardolph Sewerage Disposal Works was established by Marriott Ogle Tarbotton as part of Nottingham's modern sewerage system.

person_connections.connection: Established Stoke Bardolph Sewerage Disposal Works as part of Nottingham's modern sewerage system
